agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to study 2026-27 operating budget and 5-year capital plan
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will hold a study session on the proposed FY 2026-27 Operating Budget ($324.7M general fund revenue) and FY 2026-31 Five-Year Capital Improvement Plan ($174M total). Departments presenting: City Attorney, City Clerk, City Manager, Fire, and Internal Services. The proposed budget is balanced; any new council requests require corresponding cuts. Key revenue changes: sales tax up 5.4%, utility users tax up 6.2%, licenses/permits/fees up 6.3%. Final adoption scheduled for June 23, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for final budget adoption on June 23, 2026 — proposed changes to business license taxes (based on gross receipts per Measure U) and other fees may affect your costs.

key_dates.label: Budget study session — council provides direction on proposed FY 2026-27 budget

Final budget adoption — FY 2026-27 operating budget and FY 2026-31 CIP

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $110K legal research contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving a three-year contract with Thomson Reuters for legal research subscription services, including Westlaw and CoCounsel Legal. The contract would cost up to $110,000 and run from July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2029. The subscription covers 6 attorneys with WestProflex, Westlaw Multi-State Analytical, and CoCounsel Legal, with annual costs of $32,352 (FY26/27), $34,616.64 (FY27/28), and $37,039.80 (FY28/29). The contract includes a two-year renewal option. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet and will be voted on at an upcoming council meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $497,000 in youth grants
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award 14 grants totaling up to $497,000 to youth-serving public and nonprofit organizations. The money comes from the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth for the 2026-27 fiscal year. Grants range from $7,500 to $50,000 per organization, with awards split into three budget tiers: Group A (budgets under $175,000) receives $99,500 total, Group B ($175,000–$499,999) receives $147,500, and Group C ($500,000+) receives $250,000. If approved, the city manager would be authorized to sign grant agreements with the chosen groups. This is not yet final — the decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to apply for BAAQMD REPAIR air quality grants
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a resolution to authorize submitting one Catalyst Grant (up to $15M) and one Opportunity Grant (up to $5M) as Lead Applicant, and as Co-Applicant on two additional Opportunity Grants (up to $5M each). The Catalyst Grant, 'Richmond Rising Together,' would fund 600 trees, 10 rain gardens, a transit pilot serving 300 participants/year for 5 years, Richmond Moves microtransit operations for one year, solar and roof repairs for ~100 income-qualified homes, and one mobile energy storage system. Applications are due May 29, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from businesses at this stage; this is a council authorization step.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: $62,280 contract amendment for Winehaven and Shipyard No. 3 consulting
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed fifth contract amendment with Wiss, Janney, Elstner Associates, Inc. (WJE) for architectural and structural consulting services at the Winehaven and Shipyard No. 3 buildings in Richmond. If approved, the amendment would increase the payment limit by $62,280, bringing the total to $822,280, and extend the contract term to December 31, 2027. The additional funds cover structural assessment and repair design for fractured trusses and compromised beams at the Riggers Loft Building, including a $10,380 contingency (20% of the $51,900 base proposal) for unforeseen conditions, usable only with Port Director approval. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond pension tax override rate set at 0.14% for 2026-27
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on the second reading of an ordinance setting the Pension Tax Override Fund rate at 0.14% for fiscal year 2026-27 (July 1, 2026 – June 30, 2027). The rate is unchanged from the prior year and is the maximum allowed under bond covenants. The levy on all taxable real and personal property in Richmond is expected to yield $30.1 million for the fund.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decision — if approved, the 0.14% rate will be added to your property tax bill for 2026-27.

agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving on-call contracts for fire prevention plan review and related inspection services. If approved, three firms — 4Leaf, Inc., BPR Consulting Group, and CSG Consultants, Inc. — would each be hired for a three-year term, with the option to extend for two additional one-year periods. Each firm's contract would be capped at $600,000, with extensions not to exceed an extra $200,000 per year per firm. Funding comes from applicants' fire plan review fees. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on side letter with Fire Management Association on special compensation
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed side letter with the Richmond Fire Management Association (RFMA) to clarify how special compensation items (bilingual pay, longevity pay, training assignment pay, hazardous materials response pay, education incentive pay, holiday pay, and scheduled overtime) are reported to CalPERS for pension purposes. The side letter also changes holiday in-lieu pay from semi-annual to per-pay-period frequency, effective July 1, 2026, with no increase in total pay.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $250K backflow inspection contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with AAA Backflow Prevention, Inc. for on-call backflow inspection, testing, and repair services. Backflow is when water flows backward in pipes, which can contaminate the drinking water supply. If approved, the contract would have a total payment limit of $250,000 over a possible five-year period (an initial three-year term plus two one-year extension options). agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to levy special assessments for unpaid code fines
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal to add unpaid administrative citation fees and nuisance abatement costs directly onto property tax bills as a 'special assessment' — a legal tool that lets the city collect the money like property taxes. A public hearing will be held before any decision is made. This would apply to 15 specific properties with a combined $188,443.34 in outstanding fees (plus $43,294 in special assessment costs, totaling $231,737.34) for violations including overgrown vegetation, accumulation, inoperable vehicles, and homeless encampment abatement. Properties include vacant lots and structures at addresses such as 251 17th St, S 28th St, 562 6th St, 434 Bissell Ave, 722 7th St, 926 7th St, 103 S 42nd St, 418 4th St, 2839 Hoffman Blvd, 4839 State Ct, and Vale Rd & Howard St. Owners must submit written objections to the City Clerk at least two days before the hearing (by May 17, 2026) to be heard.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written objections to the City Clerk by May 17, 2026, if you own one of the 15 listed properties and wish to challenge the assessment at the May 19 public hearing.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal to direct staff to explore funding for scholarships for Richmond youth. agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is considering zoning and General Plan amendments to implement 11 Housing Element programs.
